mnemonics.title: V4 Elements Rule
mnemonics.content: Think 'E-A-B-C' — Everyone Ate Bad Coffee! Each non-identity element squared gives E, and the product of any two gives the third: AB=C, BC=A, CA=B.

mnemonics.title: Non-Cyclic Remember
mnemonics.content: V4 is NOT cyclic because 'No Single Generator Creates Four' — each non-identity element only generates itself and identity (order 2), not all four.

mnemonics.title: Subgroup Count
mnemonics.content: V4 has 5 subgroups — think 'T-P-E-C-V': Trivial, Three Proper subgroups of order 2, and V4 itself.

acronyms.usage: The standard notation for Klein's four-element group in abstract algebra.

acronyms.expansion: Direct Product of Z2 with itself (Cyclic group of order 2)
acronyms.usage: The isomorphic form showing V4's structure as Cartesian product of two 2-element groups.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 4×4 grid with identity on diagonal. Picture it like a multiplication table that's symmetric across the main diagonal — like a mirror reflecting itself (abelian symmetry).
Picture a rectangle. Draw four positions: original (identity), flipped vertically, flipped horizontally, rotated 180°. These four symmetries form V4.
Think of three switches that are either OFF (e) or ON. Each switch toggles between two states (order 2). The three switches together give 2³=8 states, but V4 uses only 4.
Imagine two independent coins, each with heads (1) and tails (0). The four combinations (HH, HT, TH, TT) form Z2 × Z2, just like V4's four elements.

analogies.analogy: Like a committee where no single person can represent everyone — you need at least two representatives (any two of a, b, c) to cover all four members.
Think of three light switches controlling the same light. Each switch toggles between on/off, but combined they create only two states — this relates to elements having order 2.
Like a family of four where each person is their own 'opposite' — no one can be doubled to get someone else, but any two distinct can 'cancel' to get the third.
Like addition of even numbers: 2+4 = 4+2. The order doesn't matter — the result is the same, which is why the Cayley table is mirror-symmetric.
